label: Duty of Care
scenario_patterns: A government agency issues an approval that a private party relies on to its detriment
An auditor certifies financial statements that a third-party investor relies upon
A school fails to supervise students during an excursion, resulting in injury
A statutory board's negligent inspection causes economic loss to a downstream purchaser
legal_tests: Threshold question: factual foreseeability — was it foreseeable that the defendant's carelessness could cause harm to the plaintiff?
Stage 1 (Proximity): sufficient legal proximity between the parties based on physical, circumstantial, or causal closeness, voluntary assumption of responsibility, or reliance
Stage 2 (Policy): are there policy considerations that negate, reduce, or limit the scope of the duty, the class of persons to whom it is owed, or the damages recoverable?
The Spandeck test is a single unified test applicable to all claims including pure economic loss, personal injury, and property damage
common_fact_patterns: Pure economic loss claims — whether a duty extends beyond physical damage to financial harm
Negligent misstatement relied upon by a third party not in a direct contractual relationship
Public authority liability — whether a statutory body owes a private law duty of care
Omissions — whether a defendant who failed to act owes a duty to prevent harm
Employer-employee relationship giving rise to a duty to provide a safe system of work
sg_case_references: Spandeck Engineering (S) Pte Ltd v Defence Science & Technology Agency [2007] SGCA 37 — the seminal case establishing the unified two-stage test
Anwar Patrick Adrian v Ng Chong & Hue LLC [2014] SGCA 34 — solicitor's duty of care to third-party beneficiaries
Animal Concerns Research & Education Society v Tan Boon Kwee [2011] SGCA 2 — proximity analysis in the context of a charity and its officer
RSP Architects Planners & Engineers v Management Corporation Strata Title Plan No 1075 [1999] SGCA 53 — duty of care in construction defect cases
defences: No factual foreseeability of harm to the plaintiff
Insufficient proximity — no direct relationship, assumption of responsibility, or reliance
Policy considerations negating duty — indeterminate liability, floodgates, inconsistency with statutory scheme
The damage claimed falls outside the scope of any duty owed
